triplets.head: hips
triplets.proposition: the hips were designed to handle rotation

triplets.head: pelvis
triplets.proposition: the pelvis counter-rotates to facilitate stride length

triplets.head: hip
triplets.proposition: the hip must internally rotate to maintain proper form

triplets.head: dynamic hip flexor stretch
triplets.proposition: a dynamic hip flexor stretch can be performed to improve internal rotation

triplets.head: 90-90 position exercise
triplets.proposition: regular practice of these exercises can help to improve squat mechanics

triplets.head: improper hip rotation
triplets.proposition: improper hip rotation can result in additional stress on the lower back

triplets.head: improper hip rotation
triplets.proposition: improper hip rotation can result in additional stress on the knees

triplets.head: improper hip rotation

triplets.head: leg raises
triplets.proposition: Dr. Rob Jones advises against performing leg raises with the leg in front of the body, as this movement primarily targets the tiny muscle called the TFL in the hip flexor, rather than the glutes.

triplets.head: leg raise
triplets.proposition: A leg raise can be a beneficial exercise, but it is essential to perform it correctly to reap its benefits.

triplets.head: band around the ankle
triplets.proposition: To effectively target the glutes, Dr. Rob Jones recommends using a band around the ankle and stepping over it with the other foot, which creates an angle that allows for proper glute engagement.

triplets.head: leg elevation
triplets.proposition: The viewer should tension their core, support themselves, and then elevate their leg to work the glute medius.

triplets.head: kicking the leg back
triplets.proposition: By bending forward and kicking the leg back, the glute max can also be targeted, putting it on a stretch.

triplets.head: proper form and technique
triplets.proposition: The goal of this exercise is to help a person get better, not older, by strengthening their glutes through proper form and technique.

triplets.head: Dr. Rob Jones
triplets.proposition: To achieve this, Dr. Rob Jones recommends doing 12-15 reps for 3-4 sets.

triplets.head: Kelly Matthews
triplets.proposition: Kelly Matthews is going to go over a conventional deadlift, and that is a square stance.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: Kelly Matthews is going to go over a conventional deadlift, and that is a square stance.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: The viewer should start with their feet about hips-width distance apart, and the bar should be pretty much directly over the laces of their shoes.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: The viewer should start with their feet about hips-width distance apart, and the bar should be pretty much directly over the laces of their shoes.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: Kelly Matthews would start with an overhand grip, as it gets heavier if a person has a hard time with their grip.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: A mixed grip can be used, where one palm is in and one palm is out, or some lifting straps can be used.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: The goal for the deadlift should be to keep a pretty straight bar path, with the bar moving straight up and down, without migrating out or deviating from that one single line straight up.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.tail: shoulders slightly out in front of bar
triplets.proposition: The shoulders should be slightly out in front of the bar, as they should not be behind the bar, which would make it hard to get leverage.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: There is a big emphasis in deadlifting on keeping a really flat back, and when learning, that is a really good place to start.

triplets.head: deadlift
triplets.proposition: However, when doing practice reps or submaximal efforts, keeping a neutral spine is a good place to be.
